items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.q: How many essential amino acids are there for adults?
items.why: It's 9. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a HBV protein source?
items.why: Correct answer: soya. “wheat” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many kcal does 1 g of protein provide?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 kcal/g. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
